preFix.title: Clasped warp colour gradients
preFix.subtitle: Introducing a sharp or blended colour transition across the warp width without two separate chains
preFix.excerpt: A clasped warp joins two threads from different colour cones at one selvedge and draws them through the heddle as a paired end. Where the two colours meet at the clasp point, the warp transitions from one colour to the other across the width. One chain, two colours, adjustable transition zone.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from modern rigid-heddle technique instruction. The clasped warp method is an observation-led development documented in contemporary rigid-heddle workshops and published manuals; no single source is followed verbatim.

content.text: The
clasped warp

is a direct-warp technique. It is worked on the loom rather than on a warping board. The colour transition point is set by where you place the loop joining the two cones. It produces a warp with a colour gradient across the width. No rearrangement of threads, extra beaming passes, or colour-mixing in the dye pot is needed.
The trade-off is that the clasp
selvedge
carries slightly more bulk than the opposite selvedge. The
sett
calculation must treat the clasp zone as double-threaded at the first slot or hole.
What you need

attrs.name: Rigid heddle loom, 40 cm or wider
Two cones or balls of yarn in contrasting colours, same weight and fibre
Weft yarn: neutral or either of the two warp colours

content.text: Step 1: Set the clasp point

Decide where across the warp width the colour transition should occur. For a centred gradient, the clasp point is at the middle of the heddle. For an off-centre gradient, set the clasp point at a third or quarter of the width. Mark the clasp point on the heddle with a piece of contrasting yarn looped through a slot.

The clasp point marks where colour A ends and colour B begins across the width. To the left of the clasp point, the warp will be colour A. To the right, colour B. If the clasp is at the exact centre slot, the two halves will be equal width.
Step 2: Direct warp with two cones
Use the direct-warp method (warping from the back beam peg to the front apron rod while threading each end through a slot as you go). Begin at the right selvedge with colour B.
Pull a length of colour B from the cone, loop it around the back peg, and thread the loop through the first slot at the right selvedge. Draw both legs of the loop down to the front apron rod.
Continue threading colour B from right to left, one slot at a time, until you reach the clasp point.

At the clasp point slot, bring a length of colour A and loop it through the same slot alongside the last colour B thread. Pull the loop of colour A until the tail is at the left selvedge and the loop itself is at the clasp point. The two threads (one B, one A) now pass through the clasp point slot as a pair.
Continue threading colour A from the clasp point to the left selvedge, one slot at a time as before.

attrs.title: Two threads, one slot at the clasp

content.text: The clasp slot holds two thread legs: the last leg of colour B and the first leg of colour A. This doubles the thread count at that slot and will show as a very slightly wider rib in the cloth. Placing the clasp at a point where the two colours overlap by one slot each creates a blended zone rather than a sharp line.
Step 3: Thread the holes

After threading all the slots, transfer alternate threads from slots to holes as usual for a rigid heddle direct warp. At the clasp point, the two threads sitting in that slot should be separated: put one through the hole above and leave one in the slot. This restores a single-thread-per-position count at the clasp zone and produces a clean interlacement at that edge of the colour transition.
How the clasped section affects sett and cloth appearance

Once both threads are separated into their own slot and hole positions at the clasp point, the sett is consistent across the full width. The clause zone does not create a denser area in the finished cloth provided the thread separation is done correctly. The only visible effect at the clasp point is the colour transition itself.

For a sharp colour transition, keep the clasp zone to a single slot. For a blended zone, allow two or three slots at the clasp point to carry threads of both colours: the intermingling of the two colours in adjacent slots creates a narrow strip of mixed colour where the two warp colours intersect in the weave structure.
Weaving the clasped warp

Weave as normal. A neutral weft colour will let both warp colours read clearly and make the gradient across the width the primary visual feature. A weft that matches one of the warp colours will appear to blend with that half of the warp while contrasting with the other half, creating a more complex visual interaction.

Watch the clasp selvedge for draw-in. Because the two colour B threads are looped around the back peg together, there is slightly more tension on colour B at the back beam. If the clasp selvedge is pulling in, ease the colour B threads back at the back peg to equalise the tension before the next advance.

attrs.fix: Stop and re-thread the clasp zone. Separate the two threads into their own positions: one in the slot and one in the adjacent hole. This distributes the double thread count correctly and removes the ridge.
attrs.cause: Two threads from both colours are sharing one slot without being separated into slot and hole at the clasp point
attrs.problem: The colour transition shows as a visible ridge or line in the weave structure

attrs.fix: Check the back-beam peg tension. If colour A is looser, wind more of the colour A chain around the peg before tying on. If the difference is more than 1-2 cm slack, re-dress that section of the warp.
attrs.cause: The two cones are feeding at different rates, or one yarn stretched slightly during warping
attrs.problem: One half of the warp is noticeably looser than the other
